sound_type: coordinated duet vocalizations -- male and female calling in synchronized territorial displays
ecosystem: Open forest. Territorial. Pair-bonded ravens defending territory through coordinated vocal display.
season: Year-round. Duets most intense during breeding season and territorial disputes.

coordinates.description: Reference for PNW raven territory. Open forest, mountain ridges, coastal cliffs.

taxonomy_note.description: SPECIES PAIR (100-101). Second consecutive Corvidae. Same species as degree 100, different acoustic framing. Third species-pair in the engine (Flicker 21-22, Beaver 61-62, Raven 100-101). Solo voice at 100, coordinated duet at 101.
taxonomy_note.infraclass_note: Same species as degree 100. Species-pair entry.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Same as degree 100. Corvus from Latin ('raven'). corax from Greek ('raven').
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Corvus -- same as degree 100. The raven pair is two individuals of the same species.
taxonomy_note.class_note: Aves -- second consecutive bird entry (100-101). Aves returned at degree 100.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Corvidae second consecutive (100-101). First consecutive same-family since Beaver (61-62).

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Male: 200-800 Hz (deep cronk range). Female: 250-1000 Hz (slightly higher). Combined duet spans both ranges with harmonic interplay.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: Combined duet: 300-800 Hz. The overlapping frequency ranges of male and female create a richer combined energy band.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Variable. Duets occur during territorial disputes, pair-bond maintenance, and breeding season. Can be frequent during territorial challenges.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual duet bouts: 5-30 seconds. Extended territorial displays: minutes. Alternating patterns can sustain display for extended periods.
song_metrics.note_structure: Coordinated: synchronized, alternating, and overlapping calls. Male and female contribute different tones creating complex combined structure.
song_metrics.note_types: Synchronized cronks, alternating calls, overlapping bell-like tones, coordinated gargling, harmonic interplay between male (deeper) and female (higher) voices.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Duet structure: synchronized calling (both together), alternating exchange (male-female-male-female), overlapping calls (staggered timing). The structure varies by context.
song_metrics.timbre: Combined: richer than either voice alone. Male deep cronk plus female slightly higher tones create a two-register timbre. Harmonic interplay adds complexity.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=5. Two voices louder than one. Synchronization amplifies perceived impact. Greater persistence through alternation. Combined territorial force.
song_metrics.tempo: Variable. Rapid alternation during intense territorial disputes. Slower, more deliberate exchanges during pair-bond maintenance.
song_metrics.context: TERRITORIAL DEFENSE (primary). Pair-bond maintenance. Coordinated response to intruders. Breeding season displays.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Pair duet coordination may vary by region and individual pair-bond history. Long-term pairs may develop more tightly synchronized duets.
song_metrics.key_property: COORDINATED DUET VOCALIZATIONS -- 101st acoustic element (species side). Male and female synchronized in territorial display. E=5. Species-pair with degree 100. Second consecutive Corvidae. Third species-pair in engine. 41st consecutive original CSV.

plumage.overall: Same as degree 100. Entirely black with iridescent sheen. In pair context: side-by-side, the pair is visually impressive -- two large, commanding black birds in coordinated display.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: Same as degree 100. When a pair: two large black birds soaring, vocalizing, and displaying in tight coordination.
plumage.flight_pattern: Coordinated pair flight. Parallel soaring. Synchronized aerial acrobatics. Tandem territorial patrol.

physical_description.overall: Same as degree 100. Two largest passerines in coordinated display.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Pair identification: two large ravens in tight spatial association, vocalizing in coordination.
physical_description.field_id: Two very large black birds flying, perching, or displaying together. Both with wedge-shaped tails, heavy bills, shaggy throat feathers. Coordinated vocalizations -- synchronized or alternating deep cronks, bell tones, and knocks. If two ravens are calling together in apparent coordination, you are witnessing a pair duet.

nodes.title: Coordinated Duet Vocalizations -- 101st Acoustic Element (species side)
nodes.description: Male and female ravens calling in synchronized territorial displays. Alternating, overlapping, and harmonic coordination. E=5. Louder and more complex than the solo voice.

nodes.title: Second Consecutive Corvidae (100-101)
nodes.description: Same family for two consecutive degrees. Corvidae is establishing territory in the engine. Solo raven at 100, raven pair at 101.

nodes.title: Species-Pair Precedent (Flicker 21-22, Beaver 61-62, Raven 100-101)
nodes.description: Third species-pair. Same species, consecutive degrees, different acoustic framing. Solo then pair. The pattern is structural and proven.

nodes.title: The Hecuba Contrast -- Both Duos, Both E=5, Both Territorial
nodes.description: Part A duo (Hecuba, electronic dance) and Part B pair (Ravens, territorial duet). Both E=5. Both coordinated pairs making noise. Electronic and biological duets.

cross_references.relationship: SPECIES PAIR! Same species, consecutive degrees. Solo raven at 100 (E=4, 30+ call types). Raven pair at 101 (E=5, coordinated duets). Third species-pair in the engine.
CORVIDAE FAMILY. Same family. Steller's Jay introduced Corvidae at 53. Raven pair at 101 is Corvidae's second consecutive degree. Both vocal corvids.
SPECIES-PAIR PRECEDENT. Flicker pair (21-22) was the first species-pair. Solo drumming then duet drumming. Same pattern as Raven solo (100) and Raven pair (101).
SPECIES-PAIR PRECEDENT. Beaver pair (61-62) was the second species-pair. Now Raven pair (100-101) is the third. Three species-pairs across 101 degrees.
TERRITORIAL PAIR VOCALIZATIONS. Both species where pairs vocalize together for territorial defense. Owl pairs duet quietly. Raven pairs duet loudly. Both intelligent. Both long-lived.
